℞ Engineering Notes · Field Report

Building a pharmaceutical ERP frontend with no backend

How a 45-screen life-sciences ERP — GST invoicing, batch traceability, quality, manufacturing, consolidation — was built as a single React SPA on localStorage, and where multi-agent orchestration did the heavy lifting.

Stack React 18 · Vite · Tailwind Persistence localStorage Charts hand-rolled SVG, zero deps Lines ~8,500
45
screens / route modules
30+
localStorage collections
3
dependencies, total
150kB
gzipped bundle
§ 01

The brief, and the honest version of it

Ambition met with a straight answer about what a frontend can and can't be.

The ask was audacious: an enterprise pharmaceutical ERP to rival SAP for Life Sciences — 27 module families spanning inventory, GST accounting, manufacturing execution, quality management, pharmacovigilance, serialization, cold-chain, and AI analytics. Frontend only. No backend. And every screen should feel real.

The engineering decision that made this tractable was refusing to pretend. Some of that checklist — E-Invoice IRP calls, PLC/SCADA telemetry, OCR, SSO, real ML forecasting — cannot genuinely function without a server, hardware, or an external API. So the work split into three honest tiers: subsystems that truly work, modules that are full CRUD registers, and capabilities that are represented but need infrastructure this build doesn't have. Naming that split upfront is what kept the rest of the project truthful.

The rule we held No green checkmark for anything that can't actually run. A localStorage frontend that claims to talk to the GST portal is lying to its user — so we said so, on the screen and in the docs.
§ 02

Architecture: a swappable database in one file

The whole app treats persistence as an interface it could replace tomorrow.

The cornerstone is src/lib/db.js — a reactive, localStorage-backed store that mimics a document database. Every "collection" is a JSON array under a namespaced key. Components never touch localStorage directly; they subscribe through a hook. The public surface is deliberately API-shaped, so the day a real backend arrives, this one file changes and nothing else does.

// db.js — the entire persistence contract
export function list(name) { ... }         // SELECT *
export function insert(name, record) { ... } // unshift + notify subscribers
export function update(name, id, patch) { ... }
export function remove(name, id) { ... }
export function subscribe(name, fn) { ... }   // pub/sub → React re-render
export function nextNumber(name) { ... }     // INV-2026-0001 sequences

Reactivity is a tiny pub/sub: write() notifies every subscriber for that collection, and a useCollection(name) hook re-renders the components watching it. No Redux, no Zustand, no context gymnastics — a Map of listeners and useState.

Pages
DashboardInvoiceProducts+ 42 more
▲   useCollection() / useDb()   ▼
Hooks
useCollectionuseDbusePagination
▲   subscribe / write   ▼
db.js
list · insert · update · removepub/subseed + versioning
▲   JSON.parse / stringify   ▼
Storage
localStorage  pharmaerp:*
Why this matters Because persistence is one module behind a stable interface, the 45 screens are backend-agnostic. Swapping db.js for fetch() calls is a mechanical change, not a rewrite.
§ 03

FEFO: the one piece of real domain logic

In pharmacy, you don't sell oldest-in — you sell first-to-expire. That rule drives stock everywhere.

First-Expiry-First-Out is the beating heart of pharmaceutical stock. When an invoice line sells 8 units, the system doesn't just decrement a number — it walks that product's batches sorted by expiry date, consuming the shortest-dated first, and records exactly which batches each line drew from.

// stock.js — allocate a sale across batches, earliest expiry first
export function deductFEFO(productId, qty) {
  let remaining = qty; const allocations = []
  for (const b of fefoBatches(productId)) {   // sorted by expiryDate ASC
    if (remaining <= 0) break
    const take = Math.min(remaining, b.qty)
    update('batches', b.id, { qty: b.qty - take })
    allocations.push({ batchId: b.id, batchNo: b.batchNo, qty: take })
    remaining -= take
  }
  return { allocations, shortfall: remaining }
}

Those allocations aren't cosmetic — they're what makes the 1-day invoice edit reversible. Editing an invoice calls restoreAllocations() to hand every unit back to its original batch, then re-runs FEFO on the new quantities. A simulation confirmed stock is conserved exactly: restore 8 → batch back to full, re-deduct 12 → spills correctly into the next batch, group total reconciles.

Bug caught in simulation The consolidation engine's first cut let inter-company eliminations exceed actual revenue, producing negative consolidated revenue. A Node simulation surfaced it before it ever shipped; the fix scaled eliminations to ~12% of group revenue and clamped them at the UI so a bad manual entry can't drive the total below zero.
§ 04

One engine, nineteen modules

The config-driven CRUD component that made breadth affordable.

Nineteen enterprise modules — WMS, QMS, MES, Regulatory, Pharmacovigilance, Serialization, Cold Chain and the rest — don't each hand-roll a table and a form. They declare a config object and hand it to one CrudModule engine that renders the list, search, filters, stats, add/edit/delete modal, Excel export, audit logging, and pagination.

// Qms.jsx — an entire quality-management screen, declaratively
const config = {
  collection: 'qmsRecords', title: 'Quality Management',
  numberPrefix: 'DEV',                // auto DEV-0001 on create
  columns: [ { key: 'status', badge: (r) => [r.status==='Closed'?'green':'amber', r.status] }, ... ],
  fields:  [ { key: 'capaType', type: 'select', options: [...] }, ... ],
  stats:   (rows) => [ { label: 'Open', value: rows.filter(...).length } ],
}
export default () => <CrudModule config={config} />

The payoff compounds: when pagination was added later, it went into CrudModule once and all nineteen module tables gained it instantly. Domain-accurate columns — Cold Chain tracks mean-kinetic-temperature and excursion duration; WMS tracks bin, zone, RFID tag and cycle-count variance — live in the config, not in bespoke JSX.

§ 05

Where multi-agent orchestration earned its keep

Two jobs were embarrassingly parallel. So they ran in parallel.

Building nineteen module screens, and later retrofitting pagination onto nine hand-built tables, are both wide, independent, mechanical jobs. Each unit is self-contained — perfect for fanning out across agents against a strict contract, then integrating and verifying centrally.

19
module screens built in parallel
~2 min
wall-clock for the fleet
9
tables paginated, one agent each
95
modules, clean build after

The discipline that made it safe: write the shared engine first (so agents extend one convention instead of inventing nineteen), give each agent a JSON output contract, then reconcile the results — wire routes, load seeds, and npm run build as the honest gate. The pagination fleet returned nine edited files; a single build confirmed all nine integrated without breakage.

The pattern Scaffold the shared abstraction → fan out against a contract → integrate & verify with a real build. Parallelism buys speed only when the seams are defined before the agents start.
§ 06

Charts and pagination, from scratch

No charting library. No table library. ~150kB gzipped for the whole app.

The first charts looked amateur — jagged polylines, flat bars. The rebuild was pure SVG: Catmull-Rom smoothing turns points into flowing cubic Béziers, gradient area fills, dashed gridlines, hover tooltips, and a niceMax() axis-rounding helper so the Y-axis lands on clean values. Five chart types — line, bar, donut, sparkline — in one dependency-free file.

// charts.jsx — Catmull-Rom → cubic Bézier, for a line that flows
const c1x = p1.x + (p2.x - p0.x) / 6
const c1y = p1.y + (p2.y - p0.y) / 6
d += `C ${c1x},${c1y} ${c2x},${c2y} ${p2.x},${p2.y}`

Pagination followed the same instinct: a usePagination(rows, size) hook returning the current slice plus controls, and a <Pagination> footer with a windowed page list (1 … 5 [6] 7 … 47). Totals and exports intentionally stayed bound to the full filtered set — you paginate the view, never the arithmetic.

§ 07

The honesty ledger

What genuinely works, what's a register, and what needs a backend — stated plainly.

CapabilityRealityWhy
GST invoicing (CGST/SGST↔IGST, FEFO, round-off)● WorksPure client logic on real data
Stock, batches, expiry, edit + restore● WorksFEFO engine + allocations
UTR duplicate alert · 1-day edit lock● WorksEnforced in the flow
Consolidated statements + eliminations● WorksP&L across 3 seeded companies
19 enterprise modules (WMS, QMS, MES…)● RegistersFull CRUD; not live workflow automation
E-Invoice IRN · E-Way Bill · OCR● RepresentedNeeds govt/OCR APIs
PLC/SCADA · 2FA/SSO · Power BI · ML forecast● RepresentedNeeds hardware / infra / a server

The measure of the build isn't how many boxes are green — it's that every box tells the truth about what it is.

§ 08

Seeding a believable past

An empty dashboard proves nothing. So the app generates three months of history on first load.

A dedicated generator fabricates ~3 months of transactions — hundreds of invoices weighted toward the parent company, purchases every few days, receipts against credit customers, stock movements, and a few credit/debit notes — all with correct tax math, batch allocations, and inter-state IGST flags. Everything downstream (dashboard graphs, MIS, GST report, ageing, trial balance) lights up immediately with data that reconciles.

A SEED_VERSION constant guards it: bump the version and every browser regenerates the dataset on next load, so schema changes never leave a user staring at stale demo data. Because it all runs in the browser, the generator was validated with a standalone Node simulation before being trusted in the app — the same discipline used to catch the consolidation bug.

Takeaway Seed data is a feature, not a fixture. Realistic, self-consistent history is what makes a no-backend app feel like a system rather than a mockup.
PharmaERP · Engineering field report · React 18 + Vite + Tailwind on localStorage
Persistence is one file. Domain logic is FEFO. Breadth is one config-driven engine. Honesty is a design constraint.
